turns.student: Hi, I'm here to help you. What's been going on?
turns.patient: Hey, thanks for seeing me. To be honest, I've been having some terrible stomach issues lately - crampy lower abdominal pain that's mostly on my left side, and it's been getting worse over the past few weeks.

turns.student: That sounds really uncomfortable. I can tell this has been weighing on you.
turns.patient: Thank you for understanding. It's not just the pain - I've also had bloody stools with mucus, bright red blood mixed with stool, which is absolutely terrifying to me.

turns.student: I want to make sure we don't miss anything important. When these episodes happen, does your body react in any other way?
turns.patient: Yes, I've also been experiencing increased bowel frequency - I'm talking 8-10 bowel movements per day, which is way up from my usual 1-2. The stool itself is loose to watery and feels really urgent, like I have to go right away.

turns.student: I know it can be scary to think about, but do you notice any sweating or feeling faint during these episodes?
turns.patient: Actually, no, I don't feel like I'm going to pass out or anything. But I do get really tired and my fatigue is affecting my work performance - it's hard for me to focus when I'm dealing with this constant diarrhea.

turns.student: Thank you for sharing that. I really appreciate your honesty. Does the discomfort stay in one place or does it spread anywhere?
turns.patient: It actually spreads to my lower back and sometimes even down to my thighs, making it hard to sit comfortably or stand for long periods.

turns.student: It must be frightening when symptoms like these happen. Any other areas of your body affected?
turns.patient: I've also been experiencing some achy pain in both knees and ankles, but there's no swelling or redness on my skin. It's more of a dull ache that comes and goes, and I'm not sure if it's related to my stomach issues or something else entirely.

turns.student: I'm concerned about you. Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Even minor changes?
turns.patient: No, there's no noticeable swelling on me - just this achy pain in both knees and ankles that's been bothering me for a while now. It's not really painful enough to stop me from doing my usual activities, but it's definitely a nuisance.

turns.student: Sometimes when people are going through something like this, they also feel nauseous or have stomach upset. Has that happened to you?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been feeling quite nauseous after some of these episodes, especially if I haven't eaten much beforehand. But it's hard to tell if that's directly related to my stomach issues or just a side effect of not knowing what's going on with my body.

turns.student: I can see this is taking a toll on you. How has your energy been? Your appetite?
turns.patient: My energy levels have been really low, and I've been experiencing unintentional weight loss of about 4 kg (9 lbs) over the past three weeks, which is definitely not normal for me. It's like my body just can't keep up with its usual needs, and I feel exhausted all the time, even after getting a full night's sleep.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers or night sweats? I know it might seem unrelated, but it helps me understand the full picture.
turns.patient: Yes, I've actually been experiencing low-grade fever (around 37.8-38.0°C) a few times per week, which is really concerning for me because I'm not used to feeling like this. It's like my body is just constantly fighting something, but I don't know what it is or how to make it stop.

turns.student: Your health really matters to me as your care provider. Can you tell me about any other medical conditions you've been diagnosed with?
turns.patient: I actually had an appendectomy at age 16, and everything went smoothly without any complications. Other than that, I don't have any known significant prior medical conditions.

turns.student: I know medications can be a hassle. Are you taking everything as prescribed, or has anything been hard to keep up with?
turns.patient: To be honest, I've been self-medicating with loperamide (Imodium) for diarrhea for the past two weeks, but it's only provided minimal relief. I'm worried that it might not be addressing the root cause of my symptoms.

turns.student: I don't want to pry, but understanding your lifestyle really helps me help you. How do you eat on a typical day?
turns.patient: To be honest, with my long work hours and high stress levels, I've been eating mostly takeout and irregular meals, which doesn't exactly make for a healthy diet. It's just easier to grab something quick when I'm in a rush or exhausted after a long day.

turns.student: It sounds like your life keeps you really busy. Do you get a chance to exercise or stay active?
turns.patient: To be honest, with my work schedule and stress levels, it's been tough for me to find time to exercise regularly. I've tried to fit in some light physical activity here and there, but it's not consistent. My girlfriend has actually been trying to get me to join her for yoga classes a few times a week, which has helped a bit, but it's still not enough to make up for the lack of regular exercise.

turns.student: I hate to ask this, but it's really important — has anyone in your family had serious health problems?
turns.patient: My older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. It's been tough for our family dealing with her condition, and it makes me wonder if there might be a similar issue going on with me, even though I'm not showing all the same symptoms.

turns.student: I'm so sorry to hear that. That must have been incredibly difficult. Do you worry about your own health because of it?
turns.patient: Yes, I do worry about my own health a lot, especially since I've been experiencing these terrible stomach issues and my sister's diagnosis with Crohn's disease has made me even more anxious about what might be going on with me. It's hard not to think about the possibility of having a similar condition when it runs in my family.

turns.student: Given everything you've been through, I want to be thorough. Do you drink alcohol? How do you cope with stress?
turns.patient: I do drink alcohol, actually. I usually have 2-3 beers most weeknights, but over the past month or so, I've increased that to 4-5 beers per night due to stress from my symptoms and work. It's become a way for me to unwind and try to forget about how I'm feeling.

turns.student: I want to make sure we're being thorough. Do you smoke or use any substances?
turns.patient: No, I don't smoke and I've never used recreational drugs.

turns.student: Is there anything you've been hesitant to tell me? I promise there's no judgment here.
turns.patient: Honestly, I've been drinking a bit more than I should be, trying to cope with the stress of all this. It's not something I'm proud of, but it feels like a way for me to escape from everything that's going on with my body and my anxiety about having Crohn's disease or whatever is happening.

turns.student: Before we wrap up, is there anything else you're worried about that you want to share?
turns.patient: I've been worried sick about what might be causing all these symptoms. I'm terrified of having something like Crohn's disease, and it's been keeping me up at night thinking about all the possibilities. My sister's diagnosis has made me even more anxious, to be honest. I just want someone to tell me what's going on and help me figure out how to make this stop.
